# Solution for 310. Minimum Height Trees

**Generated by:** gpt-5
**Date:** 2026-01-23 13:03:21
**Source:** <https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-height-trees/>

---

1) Intuition

- The root(s) of a Minimum Height Tree (MHT) are exactly the center(s) of the tree.
- In any tree, there are at most two centers, obtained by repeatedly removing all current leaves (nodes with degree 1). Think of “peeling the tree like an onion”: after removing one ring of leaves at a time, what remains (1 or 2 nodes) are the centers.
- Rooting the tree at a center minimizes the maximum distance (height) to all other nodes.

Key pattern: iterative leaf trimming (a topological-like process on an undirected tree) to find tree centers.


2) Approach (Pseudocode)

- If n == 1, return [0] (single node is trivially the center).
- Build adjacency list and degree array:
  - For each edge (u, v): add v to adj[u], add u to adj[v]; degree[u]++, degree[v]++.
- Initialize a queue with all leaves (nodes whose degree == 1).
- remaining = n
- While remaining > 2:
  - layer_size = number of current leaves
  - remaining -= layer_size
  - For i in 1..layer_size:
    - leaf = pop from queue
    - For each neighbor of leaf:
      - degree[neighbor] -= 1
      - If degree[neighbor] == 1: push neighbor into queue (it becomes a new leaf)
- The nodes left in the queue (1 or 2) are the MHT roots. Return them.


3) Python Solution (heavily commented)

```python
from collections import deque
from typing import List

class Solution:
    def findMinHeightTrees(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]:
        # Edge case: a single node tree has itself as the only MHT root
        if n == 1:
            return [0]

        # Build adjacency list and degree (number of neighbors) for each node
        adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]
        degree = [0] * n
        for u, v in edges:
            adj[u].append(v)
            adj[v].append(u)
            degree[u] += 1
            degree[v] += 1

        # Initialize the first layer of leaves: nodes with exactly one neighbor
        q = deque(i for i in range(n) if degree[i] == 1)

        # Number of nodes still remaining in the "core" of the graph
        remaining = n

        # Iteratively remove leaves layer by layer until at most 2 nodes remain
        # Those remaining nodes are the centers (MHT roots).
        while remaining > 2:
            layer_size = len(q)
            remaining -= layer_size

            for _ in range(layer_size):
                leaf = q.popleft()
                # "Remove" this leaf by reducing degree of its neighbors
                for nei in adj[leaf]:
                    if degree[nei] > 0:  # neighbor not yet fully pruned
                        degree[nei] -= 1
                        # When a neighbor becomes a leaf, enqueue it
                        if degree[nei] == 1:
                            q.append(nei)
                # Mark leaf as removed (optional: set degree to 0)
                degree[leaf] = 0

        # Queue now holds 1 or 2 centers (roots of MHTs)
        return list(q)
```

Notes on correctness:
- Each pass removes the outermost layer of leaves; the process converges to the center(s).
- A tree can have either:
  - One center (odd diameter length), or
  - Two centers (even diameter length).
- These center(s) minimize the height when chosen as root.


4) Complexity Analysis

- Time: O(n)
  - Building adjacency and degree: O(n)
  - Each edge contributes to degree changes at most twice; each node enters the queue at most once. Total work across all layers is O(n).
- Space: O(n)
  - Adjacency list stores 2*(n-1) entries.
  - Degree array and queue store up to n elements.

Why it’s linear: We never revisit work unnecessarily; every node and edge participates in a constant amount of operations overall.


5) Alternative Approaches

- Diameter-based method:
  - Run BFS/DFS from an arbitrary node to find one end of the diameter (farthest node A).
  - Run BFS/DFS from A to find the other end B and record the diameter path.
  - The center(s) of this path (middle node(s)) are the MHT roots (1 or 2 nodes).
  - Complexity: O(n). Needs path reconstruction for the middle node(s).
- Brute force (not feasible for constraints):
  - For each node, compute the height if rooted there via BFS/DFS. O(n^2) in worst case. Too slow for n up to 2e4.

Key takeaway: Either peel leaves iteratively (topological trimming) or find the diameter and pick its center(s). Both are linear and exploit the centroid property of trees.
